title: Why the return of ambition after depression feels disorienting
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
You finally want something again. And it feels wrong. 

 I remember the morning it hit me. I was sitting on the edge of my bed, staring at the same crack...
htmlContent:
You finally want something again. And it feels wrong.

I remember the morning it hit me. I was sitting on the edge of my bed, staring at the same crack in the wall I'd been staring at for months. But something was different. There was a flicker inside me, a tiny pulse of energy I didn't recognize at first. It took me a minute to name it. Ambition. I wanted to write again. I wanted to call a friend back. I wanted to apply for that project I'd bookmarked before everything went dark.

And my first reaction wasn't relief. It was terror.

Here's the thing. Depression numbs you. It flattens everything out, including the sharp edges of desire and drive. When you're deep in it, wanting nothing becomes a survival strategy. You stop reaching for things because reaching hurts too much. Your nervous system learns that hope is dangerous. Hope leads to disappointment. Ambition leads to collapse. So your body shuts the whole system down. It's a protection, not a flaw.

So when that ambition comes back, your brain doesn't know what to do with it. It feels like an intruder. A stranger in your own house. You might even feel suspicious of yourself. Who is this person who wants things? What do they want from me? Is this real, or is this just another manic swing before the next crash?

That confusion is not a sign that you're broken. It's a sign that your nervous system is trying to recalibrate. And it needs time. And patience. And maybe a little guidance.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker gets into the specific ways that trauma survivors cycle between fight, flight, freeze, and fawn responses. And that return of ambition? It can trigger a fight response that feels aggressive or overwhelming. Walker helps you see that this is just your system trying to protect you in the only way it knows how.

Your body doesn't trust forward motion

Think about it. When you've been in a depressed state for weeks or months or years, your nervous system adapts. It builds a new baseline. Slower. Quieter. Safer. Your brain rewires itself to expect less, to need less, to want less. This is not weakness. This is neuroplasticity doing exactly what it's supposed to do. It's survival.

Then one day, something shifts. Maybe you slept better. Maybe the sun came out. Maybe a friend said something that cracked through the fog. And suddenly, you feel a pull toward life again. A small one, but it's there. You want to cook a real meal. You want to finish that book. You want to send that email.

And your body goes, "Whoa. Slow down. This is dangerous."

Right? I've had clients describe this as feeling like they're standing at the edge of a cliff, looking down at a life they used to live. The pull to jump back in is real. But so is the vertigo. Because your system remembers what happened last time you tried to climb out. It remembers the crash. It remembers the disappointment. It's trying to protect you from that pain again.

Bessel van der Kolk, the researcher who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," talks about how trauma is stored in the body. It's not just in your thoughts or your memories. It's in your muscles, your breath, your gut. So when ambition returns, your body might respond with tension, with nausea, with a racing heart. Not because you're broken, but because your body is trying to signal: "I remember what happened when we wanted things before. Let's be careful."

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry. They ask a question that shifts the whole conversation: instead of "what's wrong with you," we ask "what happened to you?" That small shift can change how you relate to your own return of ambition. You stop judging it as scary or wrong, and start seeing it as a natural part of your healing process.

The wellness industry lied to you about "healing"

I need to be direct with you about something. The wellness industry has sold us a fantasy about recovery. They tell you that healing is linear. That you'll wake up one day, fully restored, ready to conquer the world. They show you pictures of people on beaches with perfect posture and serene smiles. They sell you programs that promise to " your potential" in 30 days.

It's garbage. And it's dangerous garbage.

Because when your ambition comes back and it feels disorienting, you might think you're doing it wrong. You might think, "I'm supposed to feel excited. I'm supposed to feel grateful. Why do I feel terrified?" And then you add shame on top of the confusion. You start judging yourself for not being a "good healer."

Let me say this clearly: the return of ambition after depression is supposed to feel weird. It's supposed to feel uncomfortable. Your nervous system has been in hibernation mode for a long time. Waking it up is a process. It's not a light switch.

Janina Fisher, a researcher and therapist who specializes in trauma and dissociation, talks about how parts of us get stuck in different time periods. One part of you might be ready to move forward, to dream again, to take risks. But another part of you is still frozen in the moment of collapse. That part is scared. That part needs reassurance. That part needs to know that it's safe to want things again.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She's a clinician who studied under Stephen Porges, the guy who developed Polyvagal Theory. Dana gives you real, tangible exercises for regulating your nervous system. Not abstract concepts. Actual things you can do when ambition shows up and your body starts to panic. She calls it "anchoring," and it's about finding a place of safety inside yourself before you take that next step forward.

How to be with the disorientation instead of fighting it

So what do you do when ambition returns and it feels like an earthquake? You don't fight it. You don't try to force yourself to feel excited or grateful or ready. You sit with the discomfort. You let it be there.

Here's a practice that helped me. When I feel that surge of wanting something, and my chest tightens and my mind starts spinning, I stop. I put my hand on my chest. I take three slow breaths. And I say to myself, "I notice that wanting something feels scary right now. That's okay. That's a sign that my body is trying to protect me. I don't have to act on this feeling. I can just let it be here."

Stay with me here. This is not about suppressing your ambition. It's about creating enough space for it to exist without overwhelming your system. You can want something and be scared of wanting it at the same time. Those two things can coexist. You don't have to choose.

I also learned to take tiny steps. Not the big leaps that the motivational speakers tell you to take. Tiny steps. Like, "I'll write one sentence today." Or "I'll look at the job posting but not apply." Or "I'll text my friend and say I'm thinking of them, but I won't commit to a call." Small enough that your nervous system doesn't go into full alarm mode.

Each small step sends a message to your body: "See? We can want something and be okay. We can move forward and not collapse. We can try and survive."

Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems, talks about how every part of us has a positive intention. Even the part that shuts down your ambition. Even the part that tells you it's dangerous to want things. That part is trying to protect you. It's not the enemy. It's a scared child inside you that needs to be seen and heard and soothed.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It helps you understand that the disorientation you feel isn't a sign that something is wrong with you. It's a sign that different parts of you are having a conversation. And that conversation is actually a sign of healing.

The truth about returning to yourself

I want to be honest with you about something else. The return of ambition doesn't mean the depression is gone forever. It doesn't mean you've "beaten" it. Healing isn't a destination you arrive at. It's a rhythm you learn to dance with. Some days you'll feel that pull toward life, and it will feel exciting and scary and confusing all at once. Other days you'll wake up and the fog will be back, and you'll wonder if the ambition was ever real at all.

That's okay. That's normal. That's what recovery looks like when you're being honest about it.

The wellness industry wants you to believe that once you heal, you're done. But real healing is messier than that. It's two steps forward, one step back. It's feeling the ambition return and then feeling the shame about feeling the ambition. It's wanting to run toward your life and wanting to hide under the covers at the same time.

I don't have a neat ending for you. I can't tell you that the disorientation will pass in a week or a month or a year. What I can tell you is that the disorientation itself is a sign of life. It's a sign that something inside you is waking up. That something inside you still believes that wanting is worth the risk.

And that's not nothing. That's everything.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many
  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
